headline: Pacific Nations Unite to Protect Ocean's Most Vital Corridors
meta_description: Four Pacific island nations launch ambitious transboundary marine protected area to safeguard one of Earth's most biodiverse ocean regions.
summary_short: Papua New Guinea, Vanuatu, Fiji and Solomon Islands are creating a massive ocean corridor to protect migratory marine life across their shared waters.
body:
Four Pacific island nations have committed to an ambitious vision: protecting their ocean waters as one interconnected system rather than isolated patches. Papua New Guinea, Vanuatu, Fiji and Solomon Islands announced the Melanesian Ocean Corridor of Reserves (MOCOR) at summits held in May and June, recognizing that marine life doesn't stop at national borders.

The region they're protecting is extraordinary. Melanesia's waters contain roughly 75% of the world's known coral species and are home to sharks, rays, dugongs and whales. It's also a hotspot for discovering entirely new species.

PNG is moving first, establishing the Western Manus National Marine Sanctuary—a 214,000-square-kilometer no-take zone roughly the size of Great Britain where all commercial fishing is banned. Vanuatu committed to protecting a fully safeguarded 71,000 square-kilometer marine area, while Fiji pledged to protect 15% of its waters by 2026.

Scientists emphasize that connecting these protected zones is far more effective than isolated reserves. Marine life migrates across vast distances, so a corridor design protects entire routes rather than creating "ecological islands."

Still, experts caution that success requires robust management and enforcement. Details about governance and community involvement remain sparse—a reminder that ambition must be matched with rigorous follow-through.
